coredata.dc:title: A heterojunction-engineering nanodrug with tumor microenvironment responsiveness for tumor-specific cuproptosis and chemotherapy amplified sono-immunotherapy

coredata.dc:description: Cuproptosis has recently identified as a unique copper-dependent cell death mechanism that may provide new opportunities for improving the therapeutic effect of tumor therapy through triggering efficient adaptive immune responses. However, the poor delivery efficiency and non-tumor-specific release of Cu ions would restrict the potential clinical applications of cuproptosis inducers. Herein, we report for the first time the development of hollow Cu2-xSe nanocubes as the tumor microenvironment (TME)-responsive drug delivery systems and cuproptosis inducers for tumor-specific chemotherapy and cuproptosis. The presence of Cu vacancy endows Cu2-xSe with excellent sonodynamic and chemodynamic activity. The hollow Cu2-xSe nanocubes with TME-responsive degradation behaviors are further utilized to load graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanodrugs to form GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions for achieving tumor-specific chemotherapy. The heterojunction-fabrication GQD/Cu2-xSe exhibits amplified ROS generation capabilities and improved TME regulation ability owing to the optimized electron-hole separation kinetics. More importantly, the significant increase in ROS levels and efficient cuproptosis could reverse the immunosuppressive TME and induce immunogenic cell death that stimulates strong systemic immune responses to eliminate tumors. Collectively, this work presents an innovative strategy for the utilization of TME-responsive cuproptosis inducers for tumor-specific chemotherapy and cuproptosis augmented sono-immunotherapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–f) TEM and HRTEM images of Cu2O, Cu2-xSe, GQD, and GQD/Cu2-xSe. (g–l) XRD, XPS, DLS, absorption, and zeta potential measurements of Cu2O, Cu2-xSe, GQD, and GQD/Cu2-xSe. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 3).

(a–d) Detection and comparison of the ROS yield of GQD/Cu2-xSe, Cu2-xSe, and GQDs under US irradiation (50 kHz, 1.0 W/cm2). (e) ESR spectrum to demonstrate and compare the ROS generation capability of GQD/Cu2-xSe, Cu2-xSe, and GQDs under US irradiation (50 kHz, 1.0 W/cm2). (f–i) Determination of the E g and E VB of GQDs and Cu2-xSe. (j) Diagram showing the bandgap structure and electron transfer process in GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions.

(a–h) Detection and comparison of the ROS yield of GQD/Cu2-xSe, Cu2-xSe, and GQD at pH 6.0 or 6.5. (i) ESR spectra of GQD/Cu2-xSe, Cu2-xSe, and GQD. (j–l) The capabilities of GQD/Cu2-xSe, Cu2-xSe, and GQD to consume GSH were detected.

(a, b) Optical properties of supernatant in GQD/Cu2-xSe solution after incubation at different conditions. (c) The release rate of GQD from GQD/Cu2-xSe after incubation at different conditions. (d) Photographs of supernatant in GQD/Cu2-xSe solution after incubation at different conditions. (e–h) TEM images of Cu2O and GQD/Cu2-xSe after incubation at different conditions. (i, j) Absorption spectra of GQD/Cu2-xSe solution after incubation at different conditions. (k–p) XRD patterns (k), sonodynamic activity evaluation (l–n), and XPS spectra (o, p) of GQD/Cu2-xSe after incubation at different conditions. The US irradiation condition was 50 kHz and 1.0 W/cm2. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 3).

(a–d) Measurements of the cytotoxicity of GQD/Cu2-xSe against LO2 and 143B cells without or with US irradiation (50 kHz, 1.0 W/cm2). (e, f) ROS staining (e) and live/dead cell staining (f) of 143B cells after different treatments. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 6). ∗∗p < 0.01 and ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

(a, b) Evaluation of DLAT oligomers and expression of FDX1 and LIAS in 143B cells. (c–e) Measurements of CRT, HMGB1, and ATP levels in 143B cells. (f, g) Flow cytometry was employed to measure the levels of CD80 and CD86 in DCs. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 3). ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, and ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

(a) Schematics show the in vitro anticancer therapy procedures of GQD/Cu2-xSe. (b–e) The accumulation of GQD/Cu2-xSe in tumor tissues. (f–i) Primary and distant tumor volume, body weight, and survival of mice after different treatments. (j) DHE staining of tumor tissues in different groups. The US irradiation condition was 50 kHz and 1.0 W/cm2. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 5). ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

(a)Detection of CRT levels in tumor tissues following diverse treatments. (b, c) Examination of CD80 and CD86 expression in the lymph nodes after diverse treatments. (d–f) Analysis of the expression of CD4+CD8+ T cells in primary tumors post different treatments. The US irradiation condition was 50 kHz and 1.0 W/cm2. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 3). ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, and ∗∗∗p < 0.001.
A scheme to show the preparation of GQD/Cu2-xSe for tumor-specific cuproptosis and chemotherapy augmented sono-immunotherapy.

head.ce:title: A heterojunction-engineering nanodrug with tumor microenvironment responsiveness for tumor-specific cuproptosis and chemotherapy amplified sono-immunotherapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cuproptosis has recently identified as a unique copper-dependent cell death mechanism that may provide new opportunities for improving the therapeutic effect of tumor therapy through triggering efficient adaptive immune responses. However, the poor delivery efficiency and non-tumor-specific release of Cu ions would restrict the potential clinical applications of cuproptosis inducers. Herein, we report for the first time the development of hollow Cu2-xSe nanocubes as the tumor microenvironment (TME)-responsive drug delivery systems and cuproptosis inducers for tumor-specific chemotherapy and cuproptosis. The presence of Cu vacancy endows Cu2-xSe with excellent sonodynamic and chemodynamic activity. The hollow Cu2-xSe nanocubes with TME-responsive degradation behaviors are further utilized to load graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanodrugs to form GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions for achieving tumor-specific chemotherapy. The heterojunction-fabrication GQD/Cu2-xSe exhibits amplified ROS generation capabilities and improved TME regulation ability owing to the optimized electron-hole separation kinetics. More importantly, the significant increase in ROS levels and efficient cuproptosis could reverse the immunosuppressive TME and induce immunogenic cell death that stimulates strong systemic immune responses to eliminate tumors. Collectively, this work presents an innovative strategy for the utilization of TME-responsive cuproptosis inducers for tumor-specific chemotherapy and cuproptosis augmented sono-immunotherapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemotherapy remains the primary treatment modality for cancer in clinic, but the poor targeting ability of small-molecule drugs limited the antitumor effectiveness of chemotherapy [1–3]. Moreover, frequent administration will lead to acquired resistance of the malignant cells [4–6]. These inevitable challenges mean an urgent need to explore new chemotherapeutics. To expand on the concept of chemotherapeutics from small molecules to nanoparticles, our group recently developed a novel interfacial inhibitor to bind to DNA major groove sites and induce DNA damage for cancer chemotherapy [7–9]. The pyridine N-modified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) acted as nanodrugs to inhibit DNA binding enzymes (Topoisomerase I/II) for inhibiting tumor growth, reversing multidrug resistance, and preventing cancer metastasis [7,8]. Although the size of GQD has increased to the nanometer level compared to small-molecule drugs, the fact that GQD with a size of only a few nanometers is easily cleared and metabolized by the kidneys. The confined accumulation of GQDs in tumor tissues limits their potential as chemotherapeutics. Hence, it is essential to explore a stimulus-responsive drug delivery system to load GQDs and achieve tumor-specific chemotherapy.

Cancer immunotherapy has been receiving significant attention as a promising cancer treatment [10–13], but conventional chemotherapy is ineffective in inducing persistent immune responses. In addition, due to the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME), not all patients exhibit the same response to immunotherapy [14–16]. The induction of immunogenic cell death (ICD) can facilitate the activation of dendritic cells (DCs) and the infiltration of cytotoxic T lymphocytes [17–20]. Evidence indicates that ICD effect can be brought about by reactive oxygen species (ROS)-mediated treatment strategies [21–26]. For comparison, SDT can address the shortcomings of PDT by offering greater tissue penetration depth [27–30]. Nevertheless, the effectiveness of ICD caused by SDT is significantly limited due to the low levels of ROS produced by sonosensitizers [31–34]. Hence, developing high-efficiency sonosensitizers to produce more ROS is expected to amplify the ICD effects.

Apart from exploring efficient sonosensitizers, there is a strong desire for approaches that utilize innovative forms of programmed cell death to directly enhance the efficacy of immunotherapy [35–39]. Cuproptosis is a novel form of copper-dependent cell death, where excessive copper ions (Cu+) induce cell death by inhibiting mitochondrial metabolic function [40–44]. At present, cuproptosis research is still in its early stages and must tackle various challenges. First, enhancing the accumulation of Cu ions within tumors is a key factor affecting the therapeutic efficiency [45–47]. Yet, cuproptosis-mediated tumor therapy continues to lack high selectivity, since the accumulation of Cu ions in normal tissues inevitably presents potential safety risks. Hence, it is essential to develop a stimulus-responsive nanoplatform as a copper ionophore to induce tumor-specific cuproptosis. Second, glutathione (GSH) effectively hinders the binding of Cu ions to the lipoylated components [48–50]. Therefore, in addition to achieving tumor-specific cuproptosis, the second challenge that needs to be addressed is the consumption of GSH by modulating the TME. Based on this situation, it is imperative to develop a TME-responsive Cu-based nanoplatform to load GQD nanodrugs, which can not only achieve tumor-specific cuproptosis, but also realize the consumption of GSH to regulate immunosuppressive TME, ultimately improving the therapeutic effect through triggering robust immunotherapy.

The semiconductor oxide Cu2O can act as a sonosensitizer and nanozyme due to its narrow bandgap and Cu + -mediated Fenton-like reaction [51–53]. In addition, Cu2O can serve as a copper ionophore for efficient cuproptosis, largely owing to its degradative properties [54–57]. However, the easily oxidation and degradation feature of Cu2O is also a double-edged sword, as it can degrade not only in acidic TME but also under normal physiological conditions, which could lead to cuproptosis in healthy cells. Furthermore, as a sonosensitizer, the potential application of Cu2O for SDT is restricted by the wide bandgap structure and the rapid electron-hole pair recombination [51,58]. Therefore, searching for a novel Cu-based sonosensitizer with smaller bandgap and further constructing heterojunctions using GQD nanodrugs as auxiliary semiconductors are expected to simultaneously solve the problem of non-tumor-specific degradation and low-efficiency sonodynamic activity of Cu2O.

Compared with Cu2O, the non-stoichiometric Cu2-xSe with abundant copper vacancies exhibits higher stability in normal physiological conditions [59–61]. The existence of copper vacancies not only endows Cu2-xSe with higher sonodynamic activity, but also endows it with excellent TME responsiveness [62]. Therefore, it is evident that Cu2-xSe is a suitable option for maximizing the effectiveness of therapeutic treatments through the TME-responsive release of copper ions and GQDs to induce tumor-specific cuproptosis and chemotherapy. However, the exploration of Cu2-xSe as a stimulus-responsive drug delivery system and a copper carrier for tumor-specific cuproptosis and chemotherapy has not been reported. Herein, we designed and synthesized a TME-responsive nanoplatform (GQD/Cu2-xSe) to achieve tumor-specific cuproptosis and chemotherapy enhanced sono-immunotherapy by depositing GQD nanodrugs on hollow Cu2-xSe nanocubes, which were synthesized from Cu2O nanocubes through the in-situ selenization strategy (Scheme 1 ). By examining the tumor-specific degradation features of Cu2-xSe nanocubes, the potential clinical translation advantages of GQD/Cu2-xSe can be highlighted. First, the fabricated GQD/Cu2-xSe possessed enhanced US-activated ROS generation and Fenton-like reaction activity owing to the improved electron-hole separation kinetics. Second, GQD/Cu2-xSe possessed controllable degradation behaviors, which cannot be degraded under normal physiological conditions, but can respond to GSH to specifically degrade and release GQDs, Cu+, Cu2+, and Se ions in the TME. Third, the released Cu ions specific to the tumor can trigger the cascade amplification of ROS production and induce tumor-specific cuproptosis, leading to severe cellular damage that activates ICD effects. Fourth, the tumor-specific released Se ions can directly promote DC maturation for enhanced immunotherapy. Fifth, the tumor-specific released GQDs could target the major groove of DNA, inhibit topoisomerase I/II, and cause DNA damage to induce apoptosis for the enhanced chemotherapy based on our previous report [7]. Owing to these favorable features, the satisfactory therapeutic effects of primary and distant tumors were achieved through the tumor-specific cuproptosis and chemotherapy enhanced sono-immunotherapy.

Cu2O nanocubes were initially prepared from ascorbic acid and CuSO4 in a SDS aqueous solution. TEM image presented in Fig. 1 a not only demonstrated the cubic morphology of Cu2O nanocubes, but also indicated that the size of Cu2O nanocubes was approximately 100 nm, which was consistent with the DLS result (106 nm) (Fig. 1j). Six characteristic peaks were detected in Cu2O nanocubes (Fig. S1a), corresponding to the (110), (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) planes of Cu2O structures (PDF#77–0199). As illustrated in Fig. S2, the Cu2O nanocubes that were recently prepared displayed a yellow color in an aqueous solution. However, the color of Cu2O nanocubes changed to a clear after 24 h, indicating their instability and vulnerability to oxidation in aqueous solutions, even under typical physiological conditions.

Considering that the easy oxidation feature of Cu2O nanocubes, we then synthesized the hollow Cu2-xSe nanocubes to avoid the premature Cu ion release before reaching the tumor tissues. Cu2O was sacrificed as a template and in-situ etched with Se2− obtained by NaBH4 reduction. The solubility product constant of copper selenide (Ksp = 10−61) being several orders of magnitude lower than that of Cu2O (Ksp = 10−5) is similar to the Kirkendall effect. When Se2− is introduced to the Cu2O solution, it will spontaneously form a layer of Cu2-xSe on Cu2O. The hollow morphology of Cu2-xSe nanocubes can be found in their TEM image (Fig. 1b). The good crystallization of Cu2-xSe nanocubes can be detected in the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1c), revealing a crystal spacing of 0.317 nm corresponded to the (102) plane of Cu2-xSe structure. Fig. S1b revealed ten diffraction peaks of Cu2-xSe nanocubes, corresponding to the (100), (101), (102), (103), (106), (008), (107), (114), (202), and (205) planes (PDF#83–1814). XPS analysis presented in Fig. S3 indicated the presence of Cu 2p and Se 3d peaks. The existence of Cu+ and Cu2+ with the ratio of 4.82 can be found in Cu2-xSe nanocubes. The change of color during the synthetic process also demonstrated the successful preparation of the Cu2-xSe. Fig. S2 revealed that the color of Cu2O nanocube solution was changed from yellow to black after being transformed into Cu2-xSe nanocubes. The improved stability of Cu2-xSe nanocubes compared with Cu2O nanocubes was also demonstrated, which exhibited that no significant color change can be detected after storing for 1 day.

GQDs, modified with N-heterocycles, were synthesized using julolidine as a containing-N precursor. The N-heterocycle modified features of GQDs can be demonstrated by their XPS spectra (Fig. S4), which suggested the presence of C, N, and O elements in GQDs. The existence of pyridinic N in GQDs was verified by their high-resolution N 1s spectrum, which would be protonated in an aqueous solution and thereby lead to a positive charge characteristic of GQDs. By examining the Zeta potential (Fig. 1l), it was revealed that GQDs possess positive charge features, with a recorded value of 25.8 ± 5.2 mV at a low concentration of 30 μg/mL. GQDs possess strong positive charge characteristics, allowing them to specifically target the DNA major groove in cancer cells. This targeted approach resulted in DNA damage by blocking the activity of topoisomerase I/II, as evidenced in our previous research [7]. The successful preparation of GQDs was confirmed by their high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1d), which exhibited that the distinct lattice fringe with the spacing of 0.21 nm was detected in GQDs. We also investigated the XRD pattern of GQDs, which revealed a broad diffraction peak at approximately 23° corresponded to the graphitic structure of GQDs (Fig. 1g).

Due to the powerful electrostatic interaction, the positively charged GQDs were readily adhered to the negatively charged Cu2-xSe nanocubes. Upon examination of the TEM image (Fig. 1e), it was evident that a thin GQD layer was present on Cu2-xSe. Subsequently, the high-resolution TEM characterization was carried out to further validate the successful loading of GQDs. Fig. 1f exhibited that a distinct lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was found in GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions, which was attributed to the deposition of GQDs. Besides examining morphology, we also performed structural analysis, including XRD and XPS. In addition to the diffraction peaks attributed to Cu2-xSe, the XRD pattern of the heterojunctions also showed a broad diffraction peak at around 24°, which corresponded to GQDs (Fig. 1g). The high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra of heterojunctions showed the presence of C–C/CC, C–N, C–OH, pyridinic N, graphitic N, and O–H (Fig. S5), which were similar to those of the single-component GQDs (Fig. S4). After the deposition of GQDs, the size of Cu2-xSe nanocubes exhibited a slight increase (Fig. 1j). The successful construction of GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions was clearly verified by the detection of the characteristic absorption peak of GQDs at 450 nm in GQD/Cu2-xSe (Fig. 1k).

The heterojunctions displayed a superior ability to produce ROS, resulting in a more significant reduction of DPBF peaks (Fig. 2 a). By analyzing the rate constant of ROS yield, we were able to evaluate the ROS generation capability. The data showed that the heterojunction group had a higher rate constant compared to the single-component Cu2-xSe group (Fig. 2b–d). Then, we utilized ESR measurements to further investigate the ROS generation capabilities of these samples using TEMP as a 1O2 probe. Fig. 2e illustrated the presence of three resonance peaks of 1O2 in the ESR spectra. In addition, the GQD/Cu2-xSe group demonstrated stronger resonance peaks of 1O2, which correlates with the outcomes of the DPBF degradation. Furthermore, to determine the optimum conditions for the preparation of GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions, we prepared different proportions of GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions through changing the feeding concentration of GQD and Cu2-xSe. Another two kinds of GQD/Cu2-xSe were synthesized when the ratio of GQD and Cu2-xSe was 2:1 and 1:2. Then, we compared the sonodynamic activity of the three kinds of GQD/Cu2-xSe. As depicted in Fig. 2d and S6, the ROS generation efficiency of GQD/Cu2-xSe under US irradiation was higher than that of GQD/Cu2-xSe-1 and GQD/Cu2-xSe-3.

Following that, we investigated several band structure measurements to elucidate the increased sonodynamic mechanisms. Initially, the absorption spectrum was utilized to measure the bandgap of single-component GQDs and Cu2-xSe. Fig. 2f illustrates that the E g of Cu2-xSe is determined to be about 1.79 eV, implying that the narrow bandgap characteristics. Furthermore, the E g value of GQDs was estimated to be about 2.41 eV (Fig. 2g). We then conducted measurements on the valence band potential (E VB) of GQDs and Cu2-xSe. The E VB of GQDs and Cu2-xSe was established to be 0.51 and 1.30 eV, respectively (Fig. 2h and i). Consequently, the calculated potential of the conduction band for GQDs and Cu2-xSe was found to be −1.90 and −0.49 eV, respectively. The surface charge characteristics of GQDs and Cu2-xSe show that the built-in electric field within heterojunctions points from GQDs to Cu2-xSe (Fig. 2j). Hence, the electron transfer proceeded from the conduction band of Cu2-xSe to the valence band of GQDs. The increased sonodynamic efficacy of GQD/Cu2-xSe is thought to result from the formation of Z-scheme heterojunctions that hinder the recombination of electron-hole pairs within the individual semiconductor.

We then delved into whether the construction of GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions could further amplify the Fenton-like reaction activity of Cu2-xSe, in addition to its already heightened sonodynamic activity. We used TMB to identify •OH generated by GQD, Cu2-xSe, and GQD/Cu2-xSe at different pH levels. With the rise in H2O2 concentration, the increased absorbance of TMB at 652 nm demonstrated the formation of •OH in the presence of GQD/Cu2-xSe or Cu2-xSe (Fig. 3 a and b). More interestingly, there was a noticeable difference in the rate constant of •OH production between the heterojunction group (0.18 min−1) and the single-component Cu2-xSe group (0.14 min−1), as illustrated in Fig. 3d. This observation indicated that by forming heterojunctions, the sonodynamic activity of the hollow Cu2-xSe nanocubes was enhanced through the inhibition of electron-hole pair recombination, while also improving its chemodynamic properties by accelerating the electron transfer process. In addition, there was no noticeable absorption change for the single-component GQDs as the H2O2 concentration increased at pH 6.0 (Fig. 3c). Fig. 3e, f illustrated that GQD/Cu2-xSe and Cu2-xSe can also produce •OH at pH 6.5. At pH 6.5, GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions, like at pH 6.0, displayed greater •OH production efficiency than the hollow Cu2-xSe nanocube alone (Fig. 3h). Additionally, it was observed that the generation of •OH was influenced by pH in both GQD/Cu2-xSe and Cu2-xSe, with a higher rate constant seen at pH 6.0 in comparison to pH 6.5 (Fig. 3d–h). Furthermore, there was no apparent generation of •OH in GQD/Cu2-xSe and Cu2-xSe at pH 7.4 (Fig. S7). As seen in Fig. 3i, the heterojunction group showed four distinct peaks for •OH, demonstrating the production of •OH through the highly effective Fenton-like reaction.

Apart from the Cu+-mediated Fenton-like reaction, we also examined if GQD/Cu2-xSe and Cu2-xSe have the potential to deplete GSH because of the existence of Cu2+ in GQD/Cu2-xSe and Cu2-xSe. The detection of GSH in the presence of GQD/Cu2-xSe, GQDs, and Cu2-xSe was carried out using DTNB after different incubation periods. Fig. 3j illustrated a significant decrease in the characteristic absorption peak of DTNB in the GQD/Cu2-xSe solution after being incubated for 8 h. Additionally, Cu2-xSe showed a GSH consumption ability comparable to that of GQD/Cu2-xSe (Fig. 3k), which could be ascribed to the presence of Cu2+ in the single-component Cu2-xSe. Since the single-component GQDs did not contain any metal ions, there was no noticeable decrease in the characteristic absorption peak of DTNB (Fig. 3l), demonstrating that GQDs did not possess the GSH depletion ability. Moreover, the quantitative data indicated that approximately 97 % of GSH was depleted by GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions (Fig. S8), highlighting the effective GSH depletion capability of the heterojunctions.

We then investigated the TME-responsive degradation behavior of GQD/Cu2-xSe. Considering that GQDs possessed the intrinsic fluorescence properties and the quenched fluorescence of heterojunctions after GQD were deposited, the TME-responsive degradation behavior of GQD/Cu2-xSe can be investigated by measuring the release behavior of GQD from GQD/Cu2-xSe. It should be noted that the selection of pH 6.0 and the presence of GSH as conditions for measuring degradation behavior is to mimic TME characterized by the weak acidity and overexpressed GSH. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, the absorption spectrum of the supernatant in GQD/Cu2-xSe solution significantly increased after incubation at pH 6.0 for different times in the presence of GSH, suggesting that partial GQDs were released from GQD/Cu2-xSe to the supernatant. Similar to the absorption spectrum, the increased fluorescence peak of GQDs can be detected in the supernatant of GQD/Cu2-xSe solution after incubation at pH 6.0 for different times in the presence of GSH (Fig. 4b). We also took photographs of the supernatant in GQD/Cu2-xSe solution to demonstrate the TME-responsive GQD release behaviors. As presented in Fig. 4d, the bright yellow fluorescence ascribed to GQDs can be observed in the supernatant of GQD/Cu2-xSe solution after incubation of 4 days. In addition to pH 6.0, we also investigated the release behavior of GQDs at pH 6.5. As depicted in Fig. S9, GQDs can also be released from GQD/Cu2-xSe at pH 6.5 in the presence of GSH. However, the release rate of GQDs from GQD/Cu2-xSe at pH 6.5 was lower than that at pH 6.0 (Fig. 4c), demonstrating that the release of GQDs possessed the pH-dependent features. More importantly, no significant release of GQDs can be detected at pH 7.4 (Fig. S10), implying that GQDs cannot be released to induce DNA damage in the normal cells.

We then investigated the TME-responsive degradation of GQD/Cu2-xSe through TEM, XRD, XPS, and absorption spectrum. The TME-responsive degradation behaviors of GQD/Cu2-xSe were carefully evaluated to compare with Cu2O nanocubes. The fast degradation rate of Cu2O nanocubes was demonstrated by their TEM images. As shown in Fig. 4e and f, the complete degradation of Cu2O nanocubes occurred not only in the acidic TME (pH 6.0 + GSH) but also in the normal physiological conditions (pH 7.4). The photographs after different incubation times confirmed the rapid degradation behaviors of Cu2O nanocubes, as their color changed from yellow to transparent at pH 6.0 and 7.4 (Fig. S11). Comparable outcomes were noted in the absorption spectrum of Cu2O following incubation for different durations at pH 6.0 and 7.4 (Fig. S12). These results clearly demonstrate that Cu2O nanocubes can be degraded before reaching the tumor sites, which not only makes it unable to achieve efficient SDT and CDT, but also leads to serious toxic effects in normal tissues. In contrast, no significant color change of GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions was detected at pH 7.4 after incubation of 96 h (Fig. S13), indicating that GQD/Cu2-xSe cannot be degraded in normal physiological conditions. Similar phenomenon was observed in Cu2-xSe, which indicated that no significant change of the absorption spectrum of Cu2-xSe at pH 7.4 after incubation of 96 h (Fig. S14). The degradation of Cu2-xSe can occur at pH 6.0 and 6.5, while Cu2-xSe cannot be degraded under normal physiological conditions. The color of GQD/Cu2-xSe at pH 6.0 became lighter after incubation of 96 h in the presence of GSH, suggesting that heterojunctions can be degraded in the acidic TME. Similar results of the TME-responsive degradation behaviors of heterojunctions were also demonstrated by the absorption spectrum (Fig. 4i and j). For TEM image, a slight morphological change of heterojunctions was observed at pH 6.0 in the presence of GSH after incubation of 48 h (Fig. 4g). Moreover, the almost completely collapsed cubic structure of GQD/Cu2-xSe was observed at pH 6.0 in the presence of GSH after incubation of 96 h (Fig. 4h), verifying that the complete degradation of heterojunctions in the acidic TME occurred after 4 days. This phenomenon can ensure that heterojunctions undergo significant degradation behavior after completing SDT in the tumor tissues, thereby releasing GQDs to induce chemotherapy and releasing Cu+ to induce cuproptosis.

The TME-responsive degradation behaviors of GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions were then demonstrated by the structural characterization. The diffraction peak of GQDs and Cu2-xSe in the GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions became weak after incubation of 96 h (Fig. 4k), which was consistent with the TEM images. XPS analysis revealed that the ratio of Cu + to Cu2+ in GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions decreased from 3.55 at 48 h to 2.74 at 96 h (Fig. 4o and p). We then verified the sonodynamic activity of heterojunctions after incubation of different times. As shown in Fig. 4l–n, there is no noticeable alteration in sonodynamic activity in heterojunctions after a 24-h incubation period. This indicates that heterojunctions can carry out SDT in tumor tissues before degrading in the acidic TME. These results indicated that GQD/Cu2-xSe can realize the TME-responsive GQD and Cu ion release, which could achieve tumor-specific cuproptosis, CDT, and GQD-mediated chemotherapy.

Following the demonstration of the TME-responsive degradation characteristics of GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions, we then explored their ability to inhibit tumor growth at a cellular level. Efficient cell uptake is a prerequisite for achieving efficient therapeutic effects with nanomedicine, therefore, we first evaluate the cell uptake ability of GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions. Considering that the intrinsic fluorescence of GQDs was quenched after depositing on the hollow Cu2-xSe nanocubes, we utilized ICG as a NIR fluorescence dye to label GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions. The bright red fluorescence signal can be detected in the cytoplasm of 143B cells after treating with ICG-labeled Cu2-xSe or ICG-labeled GQD/Cu2-xSe (Fig. S15), suggesting the efficient cellular uptake of heterojunctions. Biocompatibility of GQD/Cu2-xSe, GQDs, and Cu2-xSe was then determined through the MTT assay. The proliferation of normal LO2 cells was not affected by the addition of Cu2-xSe and GQD/Cu2-xSe (Fig. 5 a and S16a), suggesting the good biocompatibility of Cu2-xSe and GQD/Cu2-xSe. The non-toxic features of Cu2-xSe and GQD/Cu2-xSe against normal cells could be ascribed to the non-released characteristic of Cu ions from Cu2-xSe and GQD/Cu2-xSe in the normal cells. For the single-component GQDs, the significantly decreased cell viability of LO2 cells was detected after treating GQDs (Fig. S16b), demonstrating that GQDs can also enter into the nucleus to induce DNA damage, in accordance with our previous analysis [7,8]. There is no observed cytotoxicity of GQD-loaded Cu2-xSe against LO2 cells, suggesting that GQDs are not released from heterojunctions to trigger chemotherapy. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the cell viability of 143B cells decreased after treating GQD/Cu2-xSe. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of heterojunctions against 143B cells was higher than that of the single-component Cu2-xSe (Fig. S17), suggesting that heterojunctions possessed higher catalytic activity and cuproptosis effect. After irradiating by US, the higher cytotoxicity of GQD/Cu2-xSe against 143B cells can be detected compared with heterojunctions without US group (Fig. 5c), demonstrating that GQD/Cu2-xSe-mediated combination therapy possessed better therapeutic effect compared with monotherapy. Moreover, the cytotoxicity of GQD/Cu2-xSe + US on 143B cells was significantly higher compared to that of the single-component Cu2-xSe + US (Fig. S18). More interestingly, when the incubation time was extended to 96 h, GQD/Cu2-xSe + US almost completely killed 143B cells (Fig. 5d), which could be ascribed to the release of GQDs to induce DNA damage. The excellent antitumor efficacy of GQD/Cu2-xSe + US was then demonstrated by another cells (mouse breast cancer 4T1 cells), indicating that almost all 4T1 cells were killed by GQD/Cu2-xSe + US after incubation of 96 h (Fig. S19). We then utilized the confocal microscope to measure the release behaviors of GQDs in 143B cells. As depicted in Figs. S20 and 143B cells exhibited notable green fluorescence from GQDs after 2 days of incubation, suggesting that the GQDs were released at that time.

The excellent antitumor efficacy of GQD/Cu2-xSe-mediated combination therapy was then assessed by the live/dead staining. Similar to the MTT results, most 143B cells died under the treatment of GQD/Cu2-xSe + US when the incubation time of GQD/Cu2-xSe was 24 h (Fig. 5f), demonstrating the high-efficiency SDT, CDT, and cuproptosis. When the incubation time was extended to 96 h, almost complete 143B cells were killed by the treatment of GQD/Cu2-xSe + US. This phenomenon revealed that the release of GQDs after GQD/Cu2-xSe degradation could further enhance the therapeutic effect. The strongest green fluorescence signal was observed in the GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group compared to the other groups in Fig. 5e, suggesting that the majority of ROS was generated by GQD/Cu2-xSe through synergistic SDT and CDT. We then measured the ROS level in 143B cells after different treatments using SOSG as the ROS probe. As presented in Fig. S21, the strongest green fluorescence signal can be detected in the GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group compared to the other groups, demonstrating that GQD/Cu2-xSe-mediated SDT and CDT can produce abundant ROS. We also utilized the GSH and GSSG Assay Kit to evaluate the GSH depletion ability of GQD/Cu2-xSe at the cellular and tissue levels. As depicted in Fig. S22, a significant decrease of the intracellular GSH/GSSH ratio compared to the control group was observed for 143B cells incubated with GQD/Cu2-xSe, confirming their efficient GSH depletion ability.

Taking into consideration the presence of Cu ions and the TME-responsive degradation behaviors of GQD/Cu2-xSe, we explored the influence of GQD/Cu2-xSe on cuproptosis pathway in 143B cells. The aggregation of DLAT in 143B cells was clearly observed in Fig. 6 a after exposure to GQD/Cu2-xSe, Cu2-xSe + US, and GQD/Cu2-xSe + US. As shown in Fig. 6b and S23, the levels of LIAS and FDX1 decreased in 143B cells after exposure to GQD/Cu2-xSe, Cu2-xSe + US, and GQD/Cu2-xSe + US. Additionally, the groups treated with GQD/Cu2-xSe exhibited a significant decrease in ATP levels in 143B cells, as shown in Fig. 6e. When US irradiation was applied, the lower ATP level can be detected in 143B cells after treating GQD/Cu2-xSe, suggesting that the combination of ROS generation and cuproptosis could induce more severe mitochondrial damage. Furthermore, the mitochondrial damage induced by GQD/Cu2-xSe-mediated combination therapy was examined through bio-TEM characterization. After being treated with GQD/Cu2-xSe + US, Fig. S24 demonstrated that noticeable mitochondrial damage was present in 143B cells. Subsequently, the GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group exhibited the strongest green fluorescence signal (Fig. S25), suggesting that GQD/Cu2-xSe-induced cuproptosis may improve the effectiveness of SDT and CDT by causing notable mitochondrial dysfunction. It was found that GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions can stimulate cuproptosis to enhance the SDT/CDT-induced apoptosis.

Several reports have pointed out that the generation of ROS and the initiation of cuproptosis can induce ICD effects, resulting in the secretion of three different categories of DAMPs, including CRT, ATP, and HMGB1 [17–19,23]. The GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group exhibited the strongest CRT signal, indicating that the most effective therapeutic effects of GQD/Cu2-xSe occur under US irradiation (Fig. 6c and S26). The highest HMGB1 level in 143B cells was seen in Fig. 6d for the treatment of GQD/Cu2-xSe + US. The above findings unequivocally indicated that the robust ICD effects were elicited by generating copious amounts of ROS and activating cuproptosis in the presence of GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions.

Following the introduction of supernatants from 143B cells treated with various methods to DCs, the expression levels of CD86 and CD80 on DCs were analyzed. Among all groups, the GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group displayed the highest percentage of CD80+CD86+ DCs in Fig. 6f and g. Considering that Se ions can be released from GQD/Cu2-xSe after degradation in TME, we then assessed the influence of Se ions on DC maturation. GQD/Cu2-xSe heterojunctions were directly added to the medium of DCs. After adding GQD/Cu2-xSe with different degradation levels, the expression levels of CD80 and CD86 were assessed. Fig. S27 exhibited that the proportion of CD80+CD86+ DCs increased as the GQD/Cu2-xSe degraded more, suggesting that the release of Se ions can promote DC maturation.

To study the effectiveness of GQD/Cu2-xSe against tumors, the bilateral tumor models were created and GQD/Cu2-xSe was administered with US irradiation (Fig. 7 a). The NIR imaging technique was used to initially examine the passive targeting efficacy of GQD/Cu2-xSe in tumor tissues. The fluorescence signal of ICG-labeled GQD/Cu2-xSe in tumor tissues was seen to gradually increase and reach its peak at 24 h post-injection, as depicted in Fig. 7b and c. Furthermore, the ex vivo NIR imaging in Fig. 7c, d displayed comparable findings, highlighting the effective accumulation of GQD/Cu2-xSe in tumor tissues due to the enhanced permeation and retention effect (EPR) effect. Based on these NIR imaging results, the US irradiation was performed after intravenous injection of GQD/Cu2-xSe for 24 h.

Next, the effectiveness of GQD/Cu2-xSe in inhibiting tumor growth was assessed by monitoring the size of tumors. The therapeutic effect of GQD/Cu2-xSe on the primary tumor, as depicted in Fig. 7f, was thought to be a result of the Cu+-induced CDT and cuproptosis. Furthermore, GQD/Cu2-xSe also showed effectiveness in treating distant tumors (Fig. 7g), suggesting that GQD/Cu2-xSe could potentially stimulate an immune response by triggering ROS-triggered and cuproptosis-enhanced ICD effects. In addition, the primary tumors in the GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group were almost complete eradication within 14 days, verifying the impressive antitumor effectiveness of GQD/Cu2-xSe. The GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group exhibited almost complete inhibition of distant tumor growth, outperforming the GQD/Cu2-xSe alone group. Figs. S28 and S29 demonstrated that tumor cells in the GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group were largely experiencing apoptosis or necrosis. Furthermore, the mice that received GQD/Cu2-xSe + US treatment survived for over 50 days (Fig. 7i), suggesting that the enhanced cuproptosis in SDT/CDT could lead to a potent immunotherapy effect. As depicted in Fig. 7j, the highest ROS signal was observed in the GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group, indicating that the cuproptosis-amplified SDT/CDT produced more ROS than the other groups. In addition, Fig. S30 exhibited that the ratio of GSH/GSSH in tumor tissues was also decreased after the treatment of GQD/Cu2-xSe, Cu2-xSe + US, or GQD/Cu2-xSe + US, demonstrating that GQD/Cu2-xSe possessed the ability to deplete GSH in the tumor tissues.

After successfully showing the therapeutic effectiveness of GQD/Cu2-xSe, we proceeded to examine the immune reactions triggered by GQD/Cu2-xSe to understand the in vivo antitumor mechanism. We initially performed the WB analysis of tumor tissues. As depicted in Fig. S31, the expression levels of LIAS and FDX1 were decreased in tumor tissues after the treatment of GQD/Cu2-xSe, Cu2-xSe + US, and GQD/Cu2-xSe + US. These results were consistent with the cellular level results (Fig. 6b). Then, the most intense green fluorescence was observed in Fig. 8 a and S32 for the GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group, indicating the highest level of CRT exposure. This can be attributed to the effective SDT/CDT effects, along with the cuproptosis effects. Moreover, the level of released HMGB1 in the GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group was significantly higher than in the other groups (Fig. S33a). This suggested that GQD/Cu2-xSe-mediated tumor therapy may induce a more potent ICD effect through ROS production and cuproptosis activation. With the application of GQD/Cu2-xSe, the ATP level in tumor tissues was markedly elevated, highlighting the robust immune reactions triggered by GQD/Cu2-xSe, as illustrated in Fig. S33b.

After confirming the impressive ICD effects caused by GQD/Cu2-xSe-mediated combination therapy, we then explored if combining GQD/Cu2-xSe administration with US irradiation could promote DC maturation. Neither the treatment of PBS nor US irradiation had a noticeable impact on the maturation of DCs (Fig. 8b), possibly because there was a minimal generation of ROS. Conversely, the group exclusively receiving GQD/Cu2-xSe demonstrated a rise in the numbers of mature DCs that expressed CD80 and CD86, indicating the generation of ROS and the induction of cuproptosis through heterojunction-mediated combination therapy. A noteworthy increase of CD80+CD86+ DCs was observed in the GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group, as opposed to the other groups. We then investigated the possible activation of T cells by GQD/Cu2-xSe in combination with US irradiation. Following GQD/Cu2-xSe + US treatment, an increased proportion of CD4+CD8+ T cells in the spleen was noted when compared to the other groups (Fig. S34). The increased presence of CD4+CD8+ T cells was noticed after treatment with GQD/Cu2-xSe and US irradiation in both primary tumors and distant tumors (Fig. 8d–f and S35). This indicated that the combination of SDT/CDT and cuproptosis with GQD/Cu2-xSe can enhance immune responses. These findings strongly confirmed that the release of Cu+, Cu2+, and GQDs specifically in tumors could lead to the amplification of ROS generation, resulting in efficient cuproptosis and GQD-induced DNA damage that activate antitumor immune responses.

We finally evaluated the biosafety of GQD/Cu2-xSe-mediated tumor-specific cuproptosis and chemotherapy enhanced immunotherapy. The body weight of the mice remained stable throughout the treatment periods, with no noticeable fluctuations (Fig. 7h). As depicted in Fig. S36, the H&E staining images revealed insignificant abnormalities in the major organs, demonstrating the excellent biosafety and histocompatibility of GQD/Cu2-xSe. Additionally, the blood biochemistry and blood routine examination markers in the GQD/Cu2-xSe + US group remained within normal levels (Fig. S37). This indicated that Cu ions and GQDs did not escape from GQD/Cu2-xSe to cause cuproptosis and CDT in the normal tissues.

In conclusion, we have developed a TME-responsive cuproptosis inducer to load GQD nanodrugs for the achievement of tumor-specific cuproptosis and chemotherapy. The deposition of GQD on the hollow Cu2-xSe nanocubes realized the cascade amplification of ROS production and tumor-specific cuproptosis/chemotherapy as well as the activation of robust systemic immune responses through the following aspects. First, the SDT/CDT effects of single-component Cu2-xSe were improved by the fabrication of heterojunction, which could be ascribed to the optimized electron-hole separation kinetics. Second, GQD/Cu2-xSe exhibited controllable degradation properties, remaining stable under typical physiological conditions but responsive to GSH for targeted degradation, leading to the release of GQDs, Cu+, Cu2+, and Se ions in the TME. Third, the release of Cu ions targeted to the tumor enabled the amplification of ROS production through a Fenton-like reaction with Cu+ and the depletion of GSH with Cu2+. This process also led to cuproptosis in tumor cells, ultimately causing severe cellular damage that triggers ICD effects and elicits potent systemic immune responses. Fourth, the Se ions released after the TME-responsive degradation of GQD/Cu2-xSe can directly enhance the maturation of DCs, thereby boosting immunotherapy. Fifth, the tumor-specific released GQDs can inhibit topoisomerase I/II and induce DNA damage for enhanced chemotherapy. Finally, the significantly amplified ROS level and high-efficiency cuproptosis can reverse the immunosuppressive TME, thereby triggering ICD effects to induce robust systemic immune responses.
